sound_type: aggressive papery hum -- collective defensive buzzing, hundreds of hornets vibrating wings, papery quality from wood-fiber nest resonance
ecosystem: Woodland. Nests in trees, shrubs, and sheltered structures. Forested and forest-edge habitats.
season: Summer through fall (peak colony size late summer). Defensive buzzing most intense when colony is large.

species_pair.pair_type: Hymenoptera pair (92 and 93). Same order, different families. Bumblebee (Apidae, gentle drone, E=2, pollination) vs Hornet (Vespidae, aggressive hum, E=5, defense). Widest consecutive same-order energy gap.

coordinates.description: Reference for woodland habitat in the Puget Sound region, Washington. Vespidae colonies in forested and forest-edge habitats throughout western Washington.

taxonomy_note.description: VESPIDAE -- NEW FAMILY (43rd). Same order Hymenoptera as bumblebee (92). Wasps and hornets. First aggressive/defensive insect sound. Hymenoptera pair: gentle drone (bumblebee, 92) vs aggressive hum (hornet, 93). E=5.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Vespidae from Latin vespa ('wasp'). Hymenoptera from Greek hymen ('membrane') + pteron ('wing').
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. PNW Vespidae include bald-faced hornet (Dolichovespula maculata), common yellowjacket (Vespula vulgaris), and western yellowjacket (Vespula pensylvanica).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Hymenoptera. Same order as degree 92 (Bumblebee, Apidae). Two consecutive Hymenoptera entries. Order now has two families in engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- continuing insect sequence. Seven insect entries in degrees 87-93.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Vespidae enters as the 43rd family at degree 93. NEW FAMILY. First wasps/hornets in the engine.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 100-300 Hz fundamental per individual, with harmonics extending to 1,000+ Hz.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. COLLECTIVE DEFENSIVE BUZZ. Wing vibration of hundreds of hornets simultaneously.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Continuous as long as threat is perceived. Minutes to hours if disturbance persists.
song_metrics.note_structure: No note structure. Continuous, unstructured collective buzzing. Volume escalates as more hornets emerge and join the defensive display.
song_metrics.note_types: Defensive buzz (collective wing vibration), individual flight buzz (single wasp in flight), alarm buzz (guard wasp alerting nestmates).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Continuous escalating buzz. The sound has no pauses, no rhythm, no pattern -- only intensity that increases as more hornets join.
song_metrics.timbre: Papery. Harsh. Aggressive. Buzzing with a rustling, papery quality from the wood-fiber nest resonating with the wing vibrations. Denser and harsher than a bumblebee's pure drone.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Collective buzz of hundreds of hornets is LOUD. Clearly audible at 10+ meters. Overwhelming at close range. E=5 ties with cicada (89) as highest insect energy.
song_metrics.context: COLONY DEFENSE. Triggered when nest is disturbed. Guard wasps release alarm pheromone, recruiting nestmates. The collective buzz escalates as more individuals emerge. The sound functions as a graduated warning: leave or be stung.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Multiple Vespidae species produce similar collective defensive buzzing. Bald-faced hornet, common yellowjacket, western yellowjacket all produce papery aggressive colony buzz.
song_metrics.key_property: AGGRESSIVE PAPERY HUM -- the 94th acoustic element (species side). Collective defensive buzzing of a hornet/wasp colony. Hundreds of individuals vibrating wings simultaneously. Papery quality from wood-fiber nest resonance. First aggressive/defensive insect sound in the engine. E=5. Threatening. Leave or be stung.

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Social wasps, 12-25 mm body length depending on species. Slender waist (petiole). Two pairs of membranous wings. Smooth or minimally hairy bodies. Bold coloration -- typically black and white (bald-faced hornet) or black and yellow (yellowjackets).
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 93: (1) SLENDER WAIST -- narrow petiole between thorax and abdomen. (2) BOLD COLORATION -- warning colors (black/white or black/yellow). (3) SMOOTH BODY -- less hairy than bumblebees. (4) PAPER NEST -- chewed wood fiber construction.
physical_description.skin_detail: Smooth exoskeleton. Bold aposematic coloration. Warning colors signal: I sting.
physical_description.dental_detail: Strong mandibles for chewing wood fiber into paper pulp for nest construction and for capturing prey insects.
physical_description.field_id: Social wasps 12-25 mm with narrow waist and bold black/white or black/yellow coloration. Paper nests in trees or ground cavities. Identified by aggressive defensive behavior and collective buzzing when nest is approached. Warning: maintain distance from active nests.

nodes.title: Aggressive Papery Hum -- 94th Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: Collective defensive buzzing of a hornet/wasp colony. Hundreds of hornets vibrating wings simultaneously. Papery quality from wood-fiber nest resonance. E=5. First aggressive/defensive insect sound. The sound is a warning: leave or be stung.

nodes.title: Vespidae Enters -- 43rd Family (NEW)
nodes.description: First wasps/hornets in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Same order Hymenoptera as bumblebee (92). Social wasps with paper nests. Aggressive colony defense. Hymenoptera now has two families in engine.

nodes.title: Hymenoptera Pair -- Bumblebee (92) and Hornet (93)
nodes.description: Two consecutive Hymenoptera entries with opposite acoustic postures. Bumblebee (Apidae, gentle drone, E=2, pollination) vs Hornet (Vespidae, aggressive hum, E=5, defense). Same order. Widest consecutive same-order energy gap (E=2 to E=5). Gentle worker vs aggressive warrior.

nodes.title: First Aggressive/Defensive Insect Sound
nodes.description: Every prior insect sound: mating (cricket, katydid), advertising (cicada), incidental flight (mosquito, bumblebee). The hornet nest buzz is the first with AGGRESSIVE/DEFENSIVE intent. Colony defense. Alarm pheromone recruitment. Graduated warning. A new acoustic posture category.

nodes.title: Paper Nest Architecture
nodes.description: Vespidae build nests from chewed wood fiber mixed with saliva -- paper. The nest resonates with collective wing vibration, adding a papery, rustling quality to the defensive buzz. Acoustic coupling between nest material and insect sound. Architecture as amplifier.

nodes.title: The NAVVI Contrast -- Shimmer Veil vs Aggressive Hum
nodes.description: Degree 93 pairs the hornet nest buzz with NAVVI in Part A. Extreme contrast: NAVVI shimmers and whispers (E=3, dream pop); the hornet nest threatens and roars (E=5, defense). Intimate vs threatening. Widest A/B energy gap in recent degrees. The shimmer veil and the aggressive hum.

cross_references.relationship: HYMENOPTERA PAIR. Same order, different families. Bumblebee (Apidae, gentle drone, E=2, pollination) vs hornet (Vespidae, aggressive hum, E=5, defense). Consecutive degrees. Widest same-order energy gap. Gentle worker vs aggressive warrior.

DEFENSIVE PARALLEL. Both produce warning displays before attacking. Skunk sprays chemical defense; hornet colony buzzes acoustic defense. Both warn: leave or suffer. Different mechanisms, same purpose.
WING-PRODUCED SOUND. Both sounds produced by wing vibration. Mosquito whines alone (E=2); hornet colony buzzes collectively (E=5). Individual flight sound vs collective defensive display.
SHARED E=5. Both are E=5 -- highest insect energy in the engine. Cicada advertises (mating throb); hornet threatens (defensive buzz). Same energy, opposite intent.
INSECT ERA CONTINUED. Cricket (87), katydid (88), cicada (89), mosquito (91), bumblebee (92), hornet (93). Seven insect entries. The insect era extends.
